pathophysiology_summary: Pityriasis rosea is a benign, self-limiting eruption (associated with human herpesvirus reactivation) typically following a mild viral prodrome. It begins with a solitary 'herald patch' (an oval erythematous plaque with a peripheral collarette of scale, often mistaken for ringworm), followed a week or two later by a widespread symmetrical eruption of smaller oval salmon-coloured papules/plaques with a fine inward-facing (trailing) collarette of scale, whose long axes follow skin cleavage lines to give the classic 'Christmas tree' pattern on the back, sparing the face/palms/soles. It is mildly pruritic and non-contagious, resolving over 6–8 weeks (often with transient post-inflammatory dyspigmentation). Discriminators: sparing of palms/soles, absence of mucosal patches and generalised lymphadenopathy, and the herald patch/collarette distinguish it from secondary syphilis (which characteristically involves palms/soles with lymphadenopathy — serology if atypical); the inward-facing collarette and explosive onset differ from tinea corporis (outward-facing leading-edge scale); and the herald patch with absence of thick silvery scale and no preceding streptococcal throat infection argue against guttate psoriasis. Management is reassurance and symptomatic itch control, with a safety-net (palm/sole involvement, mucosal sores or systemic illness → reassess/serology).

differential.evidence_basis: Herald patch then Christmas-tree oval eruption with collarette scale after a viral prodrome.
A widespread eruption must be screened for palm/sole involvement, mucosal patches and lymphadenopathy; serology if atypical.
Outward-facing leading-edge scale and slower onset, not an explosive Christmas-tree eruption.
Thick silvery scale after streptococcal throat infection, no herald patch.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Screen for secondary syphilis and a drug eruption BEFORE reassuring; recognise the pathognomonic PR pattern.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic PR; palms/soles/mucosa clear, no lymphadenopathy, no new drug.
Palms/soles clear, no mucosal patches or lymphadenopathy — remains a serology safety-net if atypical.
Working diagnosis: reassurance (benign, non-contagious, self-limiting) + symptomatic itch control; palm/sole/mucosal safety-net.

red_flags.description: Palm/sole involvement, mucosal patches, generalised lymphadenopathy, or systemic illness (secondary syphilis / atypical severe eruption)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would prompt syphilis serology and reassessment; PR spares palms/soles.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed secondary syphilis / severe drug eruption.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reassuring PR without excluding secondary syphilis (palms/soles/mucosa/nodes) or a drug eruption.
Mislabelling as widespread ringworm and giving unnecessary antifungals.
Sending a benign PR to ED for a 'severe allergic reaction'.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Reassure after screening; symptomatic itch control (mild topical steroid, antihistamine, cool showers); GP review/serology if palms/soles/mucosa involved or systemically unwell.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring without a syphilis/drug screen, or over-escalating benign PR.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Pityriasis rosea is benign and self-limiting; treat symptomatically; consider syphilis serology if palms/soles involved or the presentation is atypical.
